# Intermission: cooking with Bayes

Welcome to the crash course on cooking with Bayes. Bayesian cuisine has many
concepts that some may find strange or exotic. Demistifying a handful of these
concepts which will allow us to prepare some delightful data analyses.

Any Bayesian model we fit needs the same ingredients:

+ Data $\bf{y}$, which here include dependent and independent variables in
a regression.
+ A model with parameters, aka a likelihood, $\pi(\bf{y} \mid \theta)$ that
describes how the variables in our data behave and relate to each other.
+ Plausible values of parameters before seeing the data, aka prior
distribution, $\pi(\theta)$. This prior distribution can condense previous
empirical work or preemptively disregard absurd behaviors or relationships.

Our goal is to learn from our data to obtain a more accurate range of plausible
values for $\theta$. This new range of values—the dish we want—is called
posterior distribution and we can write it as $\pi(\theta \mid \bf{y})$. The
recipe to obtain this posterior distribution is Bayes' theorem and is always
the same:

$$
\begin{align*}
    \pi(\theta \mid \bf{y}) =
        \frac{\pi(\theta) \times \pi(\bf{y} \mid \theta)}
        {\text{A fancy constant}}
\end{align*}
$$

This formula states that we can get the plausible values for our parameters
(i.e., distribution) after seeing our data by multiplying the two known
components and dividing by some number.

Our first impulse may be to do the math explicitly to get a specific formula.
Unfortunately, the fancy constant is so fancy that it is impossible to compute
in all but the simplest models. We could also try to approximate this formula
with another, simpler formula that is easier to compute. This works sometimes
but is often difficult and inaccurate.

Figuring out how to actually use Bayes' theorem is not even remotely obvious,
but we can gain some intuition with a short fable. Imagine that your good
friend gambling Bob gifts you his favourite pair of loaded dice, but leaves
before telling you how they work. How could you find out which numbers to bet
on when you roll the dice? A simple solution is to roll these dice a whole lot
of times and record the numbers you observe each time. With enough rolls, you
will get a good description of how often each possible number appears with
these dice.

As with the dice, we can learn about $\theta$ by repeatedly drawing random
values to get a sample. If we follow the probabilities given by the product in
Bayes' Theorem, the random values we draw will eventually describe the
plausible values for $\theta$, i.e., its posterior distribution. With this
strategy, we can ignore the fancy constant because it does not change the
*relative* probabilities of the values we sample.

Thus, we can view Bayesian computation as rolling some metaphorical dice and
then using Bayes' theorem to transform these rolls into values that describe
the plausible range of the parameters (or other quantities) we want to study.

So far we know the ingredients and the recipe to cook our dish. Now all we need
is a few details on how to use the knobs and buttons on our oven (`brms`).

## How to use your oven

From what we said above, we know that an intermediate goal when fitting a
Bayesian model is to get a bunch of random draws to describe the parameters in
our models. Let us return to the output of our regression for penguin weight by
species:

```{r return to fit_peng1}
library(brms)
options(brms.backend = "cmdstanr")
fit_peng1 <- brm(
    formula = body_mass_kilograms ~ species,
    data = penguins
)
```

First, note that `brm()` actually obtains a collection of all the random draws
we got for each of the parameters. The image below shows some colored
histograms that represent the random draws for each average penguin weight. The
black dots are the estimated means of these histograms. And the black bars
around the black dots represent 95% *credibility* intervals of the average
weights.

```{r peng1 posteriors}
#| code-fold: true
#| title: Posteriors of average weights
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 4.5
#| fig-height: 3.5
library(tidybayes)
library(ggplot2)
# Create a reference grid with one row per species
species_grid <- data.frame(species = c("Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"))
# Extract posterior draws of the mean (epred = expected prediction)
posterior_means <- add_epred_draws(species_grid, fit_peng1)
ggplot(posterior_means, aes(x = .epred, y = species, fill = species)) +
  stat_histinterval(breaks = 20, point_interval = "mean_qi", .width = 0.95) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(3.4, 5.6, by = 0.2)) +
  labs(
    title = "Chinstrap and Gentoo have similar average\nweights",
    x = "Average weight (kilograms)",
    y = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

![Posterior distributions of average penguin weights](images/post-dist-peng1.png){fig-align="center" width=4.5in
height=3.5in}

Now let us revisit the model summary we saw before (reproduced here).

```
Family: gaussian
  Links: mu = identity
Formula: body_mass_kg ~ species
   Data: penguins (Number of observations: 342)
  Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 2000; warmup = 1000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 4000

Regression Coefficients:
                 Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept            3.70      0.04     3.63     3.77 1.00     3794     2739
speciesChinstrap     0.03      0.07    -0.10     0.17 1.00     3977     2723
speciesGentoo        1.38      0.06     1.27     1.49 1.00     3844     3151

Further Distributional Parameters:
      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
sigma     0.46      0.02     0.43     0.50 1.00     3943     2698

Draws were sampled using sample(hmc). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).
```

We are already familiar with `Estimate`, `Est.Error`, and `l-95% CI` and
`u-95% CI`. But there are other, strange terms that need elaboration, like
chain, iter, warmup, Rhat, and Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS. Formal definitions of
all these terms are complicated (*very* complicated), but an informal
understanding is enough to fit almost all the models you need.

I will not detail why or how, but in most Bayesian software each
random draw needs to come from a larger sequence or **chain** of random draws.
The first few random values of a chain are usually very far away from the
probable values we want. This means that we have to let the chain **warmup**
for a while before we can use its draws.

Also, the draws from a given chain tend to be related, so they are not as
informative as if they were independent. This leads to the concept of an
**Effective Sample Size (ESS)**, a measure of the size of the sample we would
have if our draws were independent. A higher ESS means means that our random
draws are more informative, and in practice it recommended that the ESS for all
parameters be at least 100 and ideally above 400.

Finally, a chain is supposed to get draws for all the relevant values of the
parameter of interest. However, with complicated models or difficult data,
a chain can be slow down or even get stuck, depriving us of an accurate
description of the posterior distribution. To check that we are getting all the
relevant values, we should use multiple different chains, and we must check
that the chains are all *converging* to the same random values (but not in the
same order). **R-hat** (often styled as $\hat{R}$) is a convenient numerical
diagnostic for chain convergence. An R-hat below 1.01 means that our chains
converged well, while an R-hat above 1.01 signals convergence problems and,
therefore, unreliable results.

With `brm()` we can control how many *sequences* of random draws we
use and how many draws to get from each sequence. These sequences have two weird
mathematical properties





<!--
Similarly, we can learn the plausible values of $\theta$ by repeatedly
drawing random values that respect the probabilities given by the product
in Bayes' Theorem. With this strategy, we can ignore the fancy constant because
it does not change the *relative* probabilities of the values we sample. Also,
we do not have to fully compute the full product
$\pi(\theta) \times \pi(\bf{y} \mid \theta)$; instead, we can compute only one
number at the time whenever we draw a random value, which is actually faster in
many cases.

A similar idea reveals the usefulness of Bayes' theorem. Remember that $\pi(\theta)$
$\pi(\bf{y} \mid \theta)$ are probabilites, so their product is also a
probability. This means that, instead of computing,

In more formal terms, remember that $\pi(\theta)$
$\pi(\bf{y} \mid \theta)$ are probabilites, so their product is also a
probability. This means that, instead of computing,

-->
